focus: Resolve seam continuity with minimal edits.
next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 11

Paul woke at five-forty on Monday morning with the organized thought that someone needed to go through Martin's things.

Not the bedroom, not again; he'd been in the bedroom once already, Saturday morning, the CPR, his palms flat against Martin's sternum pushing rhythmically into a body that gave back nothing. His hands had been sore for two days afterward, a deep bruise across the heels of both palms that he kept pressing absently, testing. The bedroom had been police evidence, or close enough, though the deputies had cleared out Sunday afternoon and Mallick hadn't actually sealed anything. She'd told them to keep the rooms as they were. Paul still couldn't tell if she'd meant that gently or as instruction.

What he meant was the practical work. Paperwork. Martin was divorced from Linda, no children, self-employed. No HR department filing bereavement forms. No corporate life insurance packet with a named beneficiary and a list of next steps. Someone needed to find Martin's lawyer, the mortgage documents, the account information, the insurance policies. Someone needed to figure out what happened next, procedurally, to a lake house on Elbow Lake when its owner died during a fantasy football draft and five of his oldest friends had each, for reasons of their own, made the scene worse.

Paul was the right person for this. The paperwork of death was his terrain the way money had been Martin's: professionally, intimately, without romance. Enough houses after enough losses had taught him how fast grief turned into forms, signatures, inventories, appraisals. It wasn't a noble skill. It wasn't something he mentioned at parties. But it was what he had.

Paul woke in the green room off the hall, the downstairs guest room nearest the front of the house. Nate and Doug...
open_hooks_to_carry: Mallick's evidence review: body repositioned AND wiped, safe opened, voicemail deleted, medications missing — too scattered for one plan
The realization: this was a committee that never met, not a conspiracy
Mallick's comic imagination of five middle-aged men sneaking through the same house simultaneously
Her new theory: five panic attacks, not one murder — which is correct about Martin but creates the framework for solving Cliff's death later
A re-interview scene where Mallick tests the multi-tamperer theory against someone's composure
prev_tail_excerpt:
...oug still wasn't looking anybody straight in the face, but he hadn't taken back the sentence. Cliff saw the same thing Joel did a second later: whatever story they'd all been handing Mallick had died right here on the deck boards.

"I want it to stop sounding like I came up here looking for blood for no reason," Joel said. "After that, I don't really give a shit how tidy it feels."

For the first time all afternoon, Cliff had nothing smooth ready. He grabbed his beer off the arm of the chair and went back inside without offering to get anyone else one.

Nobody called after him.

A cabinet door banged in the kitchen. Then another one banged. Cliff was done pretending not to be rattled.

Paul stayed at the rail. "If she asks, I'll give her Thursday night. The affair. That's all."

Joel let out a breath that felt like it'd been living under his ribs since the dorm parking lot. "If she asks me, I'm not using Karen's name unless I have to."

"Fair," Paul said.

"And don't lawyer it," Tommy said. "Just say it straight."

Joel looked at him. "You saying that to me is offensive on about six levels."

"It can be offensive and right."

That got a brief sound out of Nate, not quite a laugh. "He's got you there, guy."

Paul rubbed at the bridge of his nose. "We need one version. Not a coordinated one. Just the real one."

Doug lifted his head. "None of us have told her the real one yet." He looked at his hands. "Just saying."

Nobody had anything to say to that.

Joel kept the bottle in his hand and let the quiet sit there. The deck ticked in the sun. The lake knocked softly at the dock posts. Nobody asked him another question.

The loons called again, farther out this time. Whatever happened when Mallick came back, it wasn't going to be the same six men handing her the same story.
secondary_character_beats: Mallick should have a moment of genuine puzzlement that humanizes her beyond the methodical detective — she's amused and bewildered by the sheer absurdity
state_deltas_required: the evidence from multiple tamperings forming a contradictory mess that no single theory explains
Mallick realizes multiple people tampered independently — she is asking the right question but will reach the wrong conclusion about Martin's death and the right one about Cliff's
